scenarios.subject: Your Instagram Account Will Be Deleted in 24 Hours
scenarios.body:
Dear User,

Your Instagram account has been reported for violating our community guidelines.

Your account will be permanently deleted in 24 hours unless you submit an appeal.

Appeal Now → http://instagram-support.com/appeal

You must act immediately.

Instagram Support Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Instagram's real emails come from @mail.instagram.com or @instagram.com — not @instagram-support.com. The 24-hour deletion threat is designed to cause panic. Real Instagram appeals are handled directly inside the app, not through email links.
redFlags: Real Instagram issues are handled inside the app

scenarios.subject: You have a new login alert
scenarios.body:
Hi,

We noticed a new login to your Facebook account from an unfamiliar device.

Time: June 14, 2026 at 3:22 PM
Device: iPhone
Location: Austin, Texas

If this was you, no action is needed.

If you don't recognize this login, secure your account at facebook.com/security.

The Facebook Team
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Facebook security notification. Facebook uses the domain @facebookmail.com for automated alerts. The email directs you to facebook.com/security — not a suspicious link — and there are no threats or demands.

scenarios.subject: You Have Been Selected for a Creator Fund Payment — $1,200
scenarios.body:
Hello Creator,

Based on your recent TikTok activity, you have been selected to receive a Creator Fund payment of $1,200.00.

To receive your payment, please verify your identity and provide payment details:

Claim Now → http://tiktok-creator-fund.com/claim

This payment expires if not claimed within 48 hours.

TikTok Creator Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. TikTok's real domain is tiktok.com — Creator Fund payments are managed inside the TikTok app, not through emails from tiktok-creator-fund.com. Any unexpected money offer asking you to provide payment information through a link is almost certainly a scam.
redFlags: Requests payment information via a link
48-hour expiry to pressure you into acting

scenarios.subject: Your account has been verified
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Your X account has been verified and you will receive a blue checkmark within 24 hours.

Thank you for subscribing to X Premium.

X Support
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate X (formerly Twitter) notification. The sender is @x.com, there are no suspicious links, no requests for personal information, and the message is simple and factual. Verification confirmation emails like this are normal.

scenarios.subject: You Have 3 New Job Offers Waiting
scenarios.body:
Dear Professional,

You have 3 new job offers from top companies waiting for you.

Salaries range from $90,000 to $150,000.

View Your Offers → http://linkedln-careers.com/offers

These offers expire in 24 hours.

LinkedIn Jobs Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. The sender domain is 'linkedln-careers.com' — look closely and you will see 'linkedln' instead of 'linkedin'. The letters i and l have been swapped, which is very easy to miss. LinkedIn's real domain is linkedin.com and real job notifications come through the LinkedIn app.
redFlags: Misspelled domain: linkedln instead of linkedin
LinkedIn sends job alerts through its own app

scenarios.subject: Your YouTube channel received a copyright strike
scenarios.body:
Hello Creator,

Your video 'Summer Vlog 2026' has received a copyright strike.

Three strikes will result in permanent channel termination, including all videos and earnings.

Dispute Strike → http://youtube-strikes.com/dispute

You have 7 days to respond.

YouTube Copyright Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. YouTube's real domain is youtube.com, not youtube-strikes.com. Copyright strikes are handled in YouTube Studio, not through external links. This is a common scam targeting creators because the fear of losing a channel makes people click without thinking.
redFlags: Real strikes appear in YouTube Studio

scenarios.subject: You have a memory from 1 year ago today
scenarios.body:
Hey,

A Snapchat memory from 1 year ago today is ready to view.

Open Snapchat to see it.

This email was sent because you have memory notifications enabled. Change preferences in Settings → Notifications.

Snapchat
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Snapchat memory notification. The sender is @snapchat.com (the real domain), it contains no suspicious links, and directs you to open the app you already have installed. Snapchat regularly sends memory notifications like this.

scenarios.subject: Action required: Re-verify to keep your X blue check
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Due to a recent policy update, all verified accounts must re-verify within 48 hours or lose the blue checkmark.

Re-verify → http://x-premium-billing.com/verify

You will be asked to confirm your X Premium payment method.

X Verification Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. X's real domain is x.com, not x-premium-billing.com. Verification status is managed inside the X app or at x.com. The 'confirm payment' step is the trap — it goes to a fake form designed to steal card details.

scenarios.subject: Your reel was reported for inappropriate content
scenarios.body:
Dear @yourhandle,

Your recent reel was reported by another user for inappropriate content.

Your reel has been hidden from search results pending review.

If you believe this is a mistake, appeal here:

Appeal → http://instagram-help.net/appeal

You have 24 hours to respond.

Instagram Trust & Safety
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Instagram's real domain is instagram.com, not instagram-help.net. Real Instagram report notifications appear in the app under Settings → Help. The combination of vague accusation, urgency, and external link is a classic scam.

scenarios.subject: alex_dev added you as a friend
scenarios.body:
alex_dev wants to be your friend on Discord.

Open the Discord app to accept or decline.

You can manage your friend request notifications in Settings → Notifications.

Discord
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Discord friend request notification. The sender is @discord.com (the real domain), it contains no clickable links to verify, and directs you to use the Discord app. Discord routinely sends these when someone sends a friend request.

scenarios.subject: Your WhatsApp will be deactivated — verify number now
scenarios.body:
Hi,

We detected your WhatsApp account is at risk of deactivation due to inactivity.

Verify your phone number to keep your account active:

Verify Now → http://whatsapp-security.com/verify

Unverified accounts will be deactivated in 24 hours.

WhatsApp Security
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. WhatsApp's real domain is whatsapp.com, not whatsapp-security.com. WhatsApp does not deactivate accounts for inactivity in this way and never asks users to verify via external web links. The goal is to steal your phone number and 6-digit verification code so attackers can hijack your account.

scenarios.subject: Your Reddit account has been suspended pending review
scenarios.body:
u/yourname,

Your Reddit account has been suspended pending review of a rule violation.

Duration: Permanent unless appealed

Appeal Suspension → http://reddit-mods.net/appeal

Admins must receive your appeal within 72 hours.

Reddit Admin Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Reddit's real domain is reddit.com, not reddit-mods.net. Real Reddit appeals are submitted at reddit.com/appeal or via reddit.com/contact. Real suspensions are visible in your account's mod-mail or notifications, not through external email links.

scenarios.subject: 10 Pins you'll love this week
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Here are some Pin ideas we think you'll love this week, based on boards you follow.

Open Pinterest to explore.

You can unsubscribe from weekly digests in Settings → Email Preferences.

Pinterest
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Pinterest weekly digest. The sender is @explore.pinterest.com (a real Pinterest subdomain), contains no suspicious links, and directs you to the Pinterest app. These weekly digests are normal.

scenarios.subject: Get verified on Instagram for just $4.99 — Limited time
scenarios.body:
Hello,

For a limited time, you can apply for a verified blue badge on Instagram for just $4.99.

Apply with payment → http://instagram-blue-badge.com/apply

Only 100 spots remaining today.

Instagram Verification
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Instagram verification is applied for inside the app under Settings — never via an external website. The 'limited spots' scarcity is fake. The form steals your card info and Instagram credentials.
redFlags: Verification only happens inside the app

scenarios.subject: Someone screenshotted your private snap
scenarios.body:
Hey,

A user just took a screenshot of a snap you sent privately.

See who → http://snapchat-notifications.net/view

For your safety, review your privacy settings.

Snapchat
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Snapchat's real domain is snapchat.com, not snapchat-notifications.net. Screenshot notifications happen inside the Snapchat app itself, never as a clickable email link. The bait is curiosity — you want to know who took the screenshot.

scenarios.subject: Congrats — you hit 10,000 subscribers!
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Congrats — your channel just crossed 10,000 subscribers!

Milestones like this unlock new features in YouTube Studio. Open the YouTube Studio app to see what's available.

— The YouTube Team
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate YouTube milestone email. The sender is @youtube.com (the real domain), the message is congratulatory with no demands, contains no suspicious links, and directs you to YouTube Studio you already use.

scenarios.subject: TikTok: Your video was removed for copyright violation
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Your TikTok video '@yourhandle/2026-summer-trend' was removed for copyright violation.

Further violations will result in your account being banned.

Review & Appeal → http://tiktok-violation.com/appeal

You have 48 hours.

TikTok Trust & Safety
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. TikTok's real domain is tiktok.com, not tiktok-violation.com. Real copyright notifications appear in the TikTok app under Inbox → System Notifications. The threat of account ban is fearmongering to make you click.

scenarios.subject: Interested buyer for your Facebook Marketplace listing
scenarios.body:
Hi,

A buyer is interested in your listing 'iPhone 14 Pro — $600'.

The buyer has agreed to pay via secure payment service. Confirm your bank routing and account number to receive payment:

Confirm Payment Details → http://marketplace-fb-buyers.com/confirm

The buyer is waiting.

Marketplace Buyer Services
scenarios.explanation: This is a Facebook Marketplace scam. Facebook's real domain is facebook.com. Real Marketplace messaging happens inside Messenger, not via emails asking for bank account info. Sellers never need to give their routing/account number to receive payment.
redFlags: Asks for bank routing and account number
Real Marketplace messaging happens in app

scenarios.subject: Priya Shah has accepted your invitation
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Priya Shah, Software Engineer at Stripe, has accepted your invitation.

Start a conversation in the LinkedIn app.

LinkedIn
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate LinkedIn notification. The sender is @linkedin.com (the real domain, with the correct spelling — note it's linkedin, not linkedln), it contains no clickable suspicious links, and directs you to use the LinkedIn app.

scenarios.subject: Your Instagram account was accessed from a new device — secure it now
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Your Instagram account was just accessed from a new device:

Device: Samsung Galaxy S23
Location: Moscow, Russia

If this wasn't you, your account may be compromised.

Secure Account → http://instagram-recover.com/secure

Instagram Security
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Instagram's real domain is instagram.com, not instagram-recover.com. The scary-sounding 'Moscow, Russia' location is meant to panic you. Real Instagram security alerts appear in-app and at instagram.com — never through external 'recover' domains.

scenarios.subject: Your Twitch payout of $487.22 is ready
scenarios.body:
Hi Streamer,

Your Twitch payout of $487.22 is ready for transfer.

Verify your payout method:

Claim Payout → http://twitch-creator-payouts.com/claim

Unclaimed payouts are forfeited after 30 days.

Twitch Creator Payouts
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Twitch's real domain is twitch.tv, not twitch-creator-payouts.com. Real Twitch payouts are managed inside your Twitch dashboard under Settings → Payouts. The fake 'forfeit' deadline is pressure to make you click without thinking.
redFlags: Real payouts managed in Twitch dashboard

scenarios.subject: Your week on TikTok: 12 videos watched, 3 liked
scenarios.body:
Hey,

Here's your week on TikTok:

• 12 videos watched
• 3 liked
• 1 shared

Open the app to see what's trending this week.

TikTok
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate TikTok weekly recap. The sender is @tiktok.com (the real domain), contains only factual recap info, no suspicious links, and directs you to the TikTok app you already have.

scenarios.subject: Your Hulu subscription has been renewed — $79.99
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Your annual Hulu Premium subscription has been auto-renewed for $79.99.

If you did not authorize this charge, click below to request a refund:

Request Refund → http://hulu-premium-services.com/refund

Refunds must be requested within 24 hours.

Hulu Billing
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Hulu's real domain is hulu.com, not hulu-premium-services.com. The scam plays on panic — recipients want to cancel the charge quickly without scrutinizing the email. The refund link goes to a fake form that steals card details.

scenarios.subject: Maya tagged you in her BeReal
scenarios.body:
Maya tagged you in her BeReal today.

See BeReal → http://bereal-tags.com/view

BeReal
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. BeReal's real domain is bereal.com. Tag notifications appear inside the BeReal app, not through external links. The scam plays on FOMO and social curiosity to make you click.

scenarios.subject: You've received a 3-month Nitro gift from sam_t
scenarios.body:
Hi,

sam_t sent you a gift: 3 months of Discord Nitro.

Open Discord and head to your inbox to redeem within 48 hours.

Discord
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Discord gift notification. The sender is @discord.com (the real domain), contains no external links, and directs you to redeem inside the Discord app you already use. Real Nitro gifts work this way.
